glossary.definition: Latin for "The Senate and People of Rome," the official emblem and shorthand for the Roman state and its claimed source of sovereign authority.
glossary.relevance: It encodes the republican fiction the emperors maintained even at their most autocratic, framing the gap between Rome's stated ideals and the reality of one-man rule.

glossary.definition: The council of Rome's leading men, originally a body of patrician elders that governed the Republic and later survived as a deliberative and administrative organ under the emperors.
glossary.relevance: Its humiliation is a recurring measure of imperial folly: an emperor's contempt for the Senate signals the erosion of shared governance and the personalization of power.

glossary.definition: The highest elected magistracy of the Republic, held by two men annually who shared supreme civil and military authority; under the emperors the office became largely honorific.
glossary.relevance: The reported jest that Caligula would make his horse consul is the archetypal image of an institution drained of meaning to flatter a ruler's whim.

glossary.definition: Meaning "first citizen," the title Augustus adopted to present his rule as primacy within the Republic rather than monarchy, the root of the word "prince."
glossary.relevance: The principate's polite fiction—autocracy dressed as restored republicanism—frames how each emperor chose to wield or flaunt power, and how far he dropped the mask.

glossary.definition: The legitimate power of command held by senior magistrates and emperors, encompassing military authority and the right to enforce law and administer justice.
glossary.relevance: It is the formal authority a ruler abuses; folly is measured partly by the distance between the imperium granted and the arbitrary will actually exercised.

glossary.definition: The elite military unit stationed in and around Rome to protect the emperor, the only standing armed force permitted in the capital.
glossary.relevance: As both shield and threat, the Guard made and unmade emperors; Caligula's assassination by his own praetorians shows how unchecked cruelty turns one's protectors into executioners.

glossary.definition: A member of the wealthy non-senatorial elite, the "knights," who staffed commerce, finance, and key administrative and military posts under the emperors.
glossary.relevance: The equestrians were a counterweight and talent pool whose alienation or exploitation by a capricious ruler signals broad mismanagement of the governing class.

glossary.definition: The standard silver coin of Rome and a primary instrument of imperial propaganda, since coin imagery broadcast the emperor's image and slogans across the empire.
glossary.relevance: Fiscal recklessness—lavish spending, debasement, or confiscations to refill an emptied treasury—is a concrete, traceable form of self-defeating misrule.

glossary.definition: The lavish ceremonial procession granted to a victorious general, the supreme public honor in Roman culture, increasingly monopolized by the imperial family.
glossary.relevance: Caligula's staged or hollow military spectacles, like the alleged "campaign" against the sea, parody the triumph and expose grandiosity untethered from real achievement.

glossary.definition: The formal condemnation of a person's memory after death, erasing their name and image from monuments, coins, and records as if they had never held honor.
glossary.relevance: It is Rome's verdict on failed rule—a society's retroactive judgment that a leader's legacy deserves obliteration rather than commemoration.

glossary.definition: The elevation of an emperor (usually posthumously) to the status of a god through a formal act of consecration by the Senate.
glossary.relevance: Caligula's demand for worship while still living inverts the ritual, a textbook case of grandiosity crossing from honor into delusion.

glossary.definition: The classical theory, articulated by Polybius, that governments cycle through predictable phases—monarchy, tyranny, aristocracy, oligarchy, democracy, mob rule—each decaying into the next.
glossary.relevance: It supplies the historical frame for reading any single ruler's folly as part of a recurring pattern of institutional decay rather than mere personal vice.

glossary.definition: Caligula's favorite racehorse, reportedly given a marble stable, an ivory manger, and—according to Suetonius—a planned consulship.
glossary.relevance: The horse is the enduring symbol of arbitrary power mocking serious office, the central metaphor against which modern follies are measured here.

glossary.definition: The law of treason against the majesty of the Roman state, expanded under several emperors to punish words, gestures, or perceived disrespect as capital crimes.
glossary.relevance: The weaponization of treason law to silence critics and enrich the treasury is a hallmark of paranoid, self-harming rule.

glossary.definition: A published list of citizens declared outlaws whose property was confiscated and who could be killed with impunity, used to eliminate enemies and raise funds.
glossary.relevance: It marks the point where governance becomes predation on one's own elite, a counterproductive purge that hollows out the state's capacity.

glossary.definition: The authority of the plebeian tribunes—including the right to veto and personal inviolability—which emperors held permanently to claim they protected the common people.
glossary.relevance: It illustrates how rulers cloaked autocratic power in the language of popular protection, a rhetorical move whose hollowness measures the gap between claim and conduct.

presets.name: Tuchman purist
presets.description: Folly is institutional: it counts only when a leader pursues a course against the available alternatives that damages the wider system, in the spirit of Barbara Tuchman's "march of folly."

presets.name: The skeptic
presets.description: Demands hard proof that an action backfired and that a better path was plainly visible at the time, discounting hindsight, vibes, and historical theatrics.

presets.name: The historian
presets.description: Weighs every axis evenly while staying alert to genuine Caligula-style resonances, treating the parallel as evidence to be assessed rather than embraced or dismissed.

presets.name: The critic
presets.description: Reads folly through the Caligula lens, emphasizing self-inflicted damage and the grandiose, capricious flourishes that echo the mad-emperor archetype.

presets.name: The defender
presets.description: Distrusts the Caligula analogy as rhetorical excess and will only score folly that demonstrably harms the leader's own interests and provably backfired.
